coredata.dc:title: Biowaste valorization into valuable nanomaterials: Synthesis of green carbon nanodots and anode material for lithium-ion batteries from watermelon seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Ashraf, Humza
Karahan, B. Deniz
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a solid biowaste watermelon seed is introduced for the first time as an alternative precursor for the production of fluorescent carbon nanodots and nanostructured activated carbon. Firstly, the seeds are carburized and then activated by using KOH after the ball milling process. The activated carbon is used as anode material in lithium-ion batteries and exhibits ∼360 mAh g − 1 capacity after the 200th cycle, with a coulombic efficiency of 98%. Additionally, carbon dots are fabricated by hydrothermally treating carburized seeds at different conditions. The obtained carbon dots show a remarkable fluorescence effect at 458 nm (when excited at 365 nm) and have the potential to be used in biomedical applications. The results show that by means of proper material selection and process design, low carbon footprint nanomaterials suitable for use in various applications can be synthesized successfully from organic wastes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biowaste
Carbon nanodots
Activated carbon
Lithium-ion batteries
Waste to wealth

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge Istanbul Medipol University Regenerative and Restorative Medicine Research Center for allowing the use of SEM and XRD in characterizations and Konya Selçuk University (as well as Özkan Üzüm) for TEM analysis. The authors also thank Alper Yeşilyurt (Arçelik) for the XRF and Raman analyses and Dr. Nilay Beğiç (Biruni University) for providing quinine sulfate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis procedure of a) Carbon dots, b) Activated carbon (sample 2).

a) Fluorescence of CDs fabricated in different hydrothermal conditions under UV light, b) Absorbance and emission (inset) curves of 7 carbon dots solution, c) Absorbance and emission at different wavelength of best yield solution (experiment 2:H2).
TEM images of Carbon Nano Dots (a, b), SAED pattern of activated carbon (c).
SEM graphs of a) Sample 1, b) Sample 2; TEM images of c) sample 2, d) sample 2.

a) XRD Pattern of sample 1 and sample 2, b) Raman Spectra of both sample 1 and sample 2, c) FTIR of both sample 1 and sample 2, d) Pore diameter distribution of sample 2, e) N2 Adsorption-Desorption graph of sample 2.
a) Voltage-capacity curvature of sample 2, b) Capacity-cycle performance of sample 2, c) CV curvatures of sample 2, d) Nyquist plots of sample 1 and sample 2, e) Rate test of sample 2.
Previously used biowaste materials and their performance as electrode material.

head.ce:title: Biowaste valorization into valuable nanomaterials: Synthesis of green carbon nanodots and anode material for lithium-ion batteries from watermelon seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istanbul Medipol University, Research Institute for Health Sciences and Technologies (SABITA), 34810, Beykoz-Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute for Health Sciences and Technologies (SABITA)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aIstanbul Medipol University, Research Institute for Health Sciences and Technologies (SABITA), 34810, Beykoz-Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, 34469, Maslak- Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, 34469, Maslak- Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a solid biowaste watermelon seed is introduced for the first time as an alternative precursor for the production of fluorescent carbon nanodots and nanostructured activated carbon. Firstly, the seeds are carburized and then activated by using KOH after the ball milling process. The activated carbon is used as anode material in lithium-ion batteries and exhibits ∼360 mAh g − 1 capacity after the 200th cycle, with a coulombic efficiency of 98%. Additionally, carbon dots are fabricated by hydrothermally treating carburized seeds at different conditions. The obtained carbon dots show a remarkable fluorescence effect at 458 nm (when excited at 365 nm) and have the potential to be used in biomedical applications. The results show that by means of proper material selection and process design, low carbon footprint nanomaterials suitable for use in various applications can be synthesized successfully from organic wastes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, nanomaterials (<100 nm) are considered one of the most prominent classes of materials because of their incomparable physio-chemical and opto-electronic features. Their attributes and multi-functionalities make them good candidates to be used in various disciplines. Among these materials, carbon-based nano-materials are getting more popular day by day. Carbon is one of the most abundant elements on earth, it can be also found naturally in various allotropic forms. The nano-sized allotropic forms comprise nanowires (fibers), nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [1]. Carbon nanodots (CDs) were first observed by Xu et al. [2] in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT). Then in 2006, Sun et al. [3] synthesized carbon-based fluorescent particles and named them as carbon-nanodots (CDs) [1]. These are nearly spherical-shaped nanoparticles of carbon consisting of sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with individual particle sizes of less than 10 nm [4]. There are several superior advantages of CDs that make them more attractive in comparison to the rest of carbon nano-allotropic forms such as having good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, electronic properties, high stability in water, low toxicity, and high fluorescence effect when excited at a specific wavelength [1,5]. Since then, CDs have become a focused subject for many researchers, and till now many studies have reported their numerous applications in cell differentiation [6,7], targeted drug delivery [8–10], bioimaging/cell imaging [11–13], gene delivery [14–16], metallic ion sensing [17,18] and catalysis [19].

The evolution mechanism of CDs involves two different approaches which are the top-down and the bottom-up approaches. The former approach involves the breakage of large particles into small nano-sized carbon-based dots, chemically, electrochemically, or physically [5,20,21] i.e., upon the use of an appropriate oxidizing agent (e.g., HNO3, H2SO4, or a mixture of both), the oxide-cutting phenomena may be responsible for breaking large carbon-based particles, resulting CDs formation. Alternatively, in the bottom-up approach, the coalescence of small atoms to form nano-sized particles is required. The latter approach possesses remarkable advantages (over the top-down approach) like being more environmentally friendly, cost-effective, practical, and having better control over the particle size of the resulting nanodots. Among the bottom-up production procedures, carburization [22–25], microwave irradiation [26–29], and hydrothermal reaction [30–35] stand out. There have also been studies in which researchers utilized a combination of approaches for the synthesis of CDs (carburizing-hydrothermal or pyrolysis-carburizing) [36–38]. Herein, the hydrothermal method is the most used one since it is simple to implement, inexpensive, and environmentally beneficial.

To date, various chemical substances have been treated to generate nanostructured materials used in bioimaging or energy-related applications (such as electrode materials). Considering the damage caused to the environment (carbon dioxide emission) during the manufacturing of nanostructured powder, it is now obvious that the rising market demand cannot be sustained by merely treating synthetic raw chemicals using primary production methods. The search for environmentally friendly fabrication is therefore strongly emphasized.

Thus, in recent years, engineering research to design the production of high-value-added products by valorizing waste has become a very important issue. The ‘waste-to-source’ idea creates a sustainable way of life in which new technologies are developed along with the environmental benefits of waste valorization. One of the biggest naturally occurring raw materials rich in carbon is green organic biowaste which includes rotten fruits, vegetables, and/or their extracts (peels, pulp, seeds, etc.). Recently, it has been reported that globally more than 50% of municipal solid waste (MSW) is organic waste generated in urban areas. By the year 2050, this 50% of organic waste is expected to hit the figure of 1635 t per year of waste generation [39]. This organic waste gives a foul smell when gets rotten and becomes the point of attraction for rats, flies, and other insects which eventually contributes to the creation and breaking out of deadly diseases. Most of the organic waste is subjected to composting to turn it into useful organic fertilizers. However, with the increase in population, waste production is also increasing, and it is not possible to discard the waste in landfills due to the lack of suitable space in urban areas. This situation enhances the efforts to reach more sustainable solutions for the disposal of biowaste. As a possible remedy for the said problems, in this paper utilization of such green organic waste in the fabrication of high-value-added products such as carbon dots and activated carbon is studied.

Up to now, studies where CDs have been produced in an eco-friendly manner using various seeds such as apple seeds [40], lychee seeds [41], acacia concinna seeds [42], fennel seeds [43], pearl millet seeds [44], linseed [45], nigella sativa seeds [46], kiwi seeds [47], white sesame seeds [47], black sesame seeds [48], sterculia lychnophora seeds [49], jackfruit seeds [50] have been published. However, no other work on carbon dot formation from watermelon seeds has been found in the open literature. We chose watermelon seeds as biowaste because they are abundant and easily available as a byproduct of the food sector, making them a cost-effective and sustainable supply of carbon for lithium-ion battery anodes. Furthermore, watermelon seeds have a distinct structure that makes them easily processed into high-quality carbon compounds. The seeds have a hard outer shell that may be removed, revealing a fibrous, porous substance that can be easily carbonized to produce a carbon material with a large surface area. Watermelon seed husk includes 39.17% cellulose, 30% lignin, and about 20% hemicellulose as major components, as well as some mineral content [51]. Because these constituents are the best source of carbon, by establishing a correct temperature profile and reaction duration, hydrothermal reaction allows significant control over the formation of carbon dots.

Alternatively, some researchers are also activating carbon issued from biowaste (i.e. seeds) in energy-related applications [52–57]. Activated carbon is a type of carbon material that has been treated with an oxidizing agent to create a porous structure. This porous structure increases the surface area of the carbon, making it highly adsorptive. It is important to use activated carbon as an anode material in lithium batteries because it has a high capacity to store lithium ions, and it also provides mechanical stability to the electrode structure. Additionally, activated carbon has high electrical conductivity and can easily accommodate volumetric changes happened in the material upon cycling, which leads to improved cycle performance of the battery [57,58]. The performances of activated carbon, obtained from various biowaste, used as electrodes are summarized in Table 1 .

Therefore, in this work, first, carburizing is applied then a facile ball milling is used to homogenize the carburized powder. After the activation, this material delivers a capacity as good as graphite and keeps cycling over 200 cycles with good retention when it is used as a negative electrode vs. Li. Moreover, by hydrothermally treating the same carburized powder at the optimum conditions carbon dots are also produced without using any additional chemicals. The emission of the dots shows that excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior is very strong in CD and by selecting the suitable excitation wavelength specific fluorescence emission range and wavelength can be achieved which may highlight their uses in bioimaging. These results demonstrate that considering the ‘waste to wealth’ idea the fabrication of low carbon footprint nanomaterials suitable for use in various engineering applications can be synthesized successfully from organic wastes.

Quantum yield calculation of the CD solution was done by using the formula given in Eq. (1) [70]. Quinine sulfate solution prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 was taken as a relative standard for the calculation:
(1)
Q
Y
C D
= Q
Y S
×
A
C D
A S
×
I S
I
C D
×
η
C D
2
η S 2

Here, QYCD and QYS are the quantum yields of the dot sample and the standard reference (quinine sulfate), for the reference solution its value is 0.54. IS and ICD are the fluorescence intensities of the standard reference and dot solution. ηS and ηCD are the refractive indices of the standard reference and dot solution. ACD and AS are the absorbance values of the dot solution and standard reference. The refractive indices of water and quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 were taken at 1.33 as stated in the literature [70].

For morphological and structural analyses high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL TEM 2100 LaB6, 200 VK) and Raman spectrometry (Renishaw Raman spectrometer 532 nm laser source, 0.45 Watt laser power, 100 - 3000 cm−1 range, 10 n sec exposure time) and FTIR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) (Pelkin-Elmer Spectrum Two, between 650 and 4000 cm−1) were used.

For evaluation of optical properties UV–VIS spectroscopy was done. Absorbance spectra were obtained from 250 nm to 700 nm and the fluorescence was measured at different excitation wavelengths (SpectraMax i3 Multi-Mode Platform, Molecular Devices).

For the preparation of the activated carbon following the procedure mentioned elsewhere [71], the powder obtained after ball milling (Sample 1) was treated with 0.45 M KOH solution at 40 °C for 6 h, then dried at 80 °C overnight. Afterward powder was heat treated in N2 environment at 400 °C for 1 h and then 800 °C for 3 h with a heat rate of 10 °C min−1. After the heat-treatment, the powder was washed with 3 M HCl for 30 min then with 0.5 M HCl for 30 min, followed by washing with deionized water until the leachate had pH ∼7, and dried overnight at 100 °C in a vacuum. Fig. 1b shows the schematic procedure followed for activated carbon production.

For morphological and structural analyses scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Gemini 500), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL TEM 2100 LaB6, 200 VK), Raman spectrometry (Renishaw Raman spectrometer 532 nm laser source, 0.45 Watt laser power, 100 - 3000 cm−1 range, 10 n sec exposure time), FTIR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) (Pelkin-Elmer Spectrum Two, between 650 and 4000 cm−1) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Brucker AXS/Discovery D8: 2θ = 10˚−90˚ by Cu Kα with 0.02˚ sec −1 scanning rate) were used. To take insight into the surface area, pore volume, and pore size, BET analysis was performed from the desorption isotherm using the density functional (DFT) method (Nova StationB, Quantachrome Instruments).

For the electrode fabrication, the slurry was prepared by homogeneously mixing the active electrode material (i.e., herein sample 1 or sample 2), Timcal-C64, and polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF binder in N-Methyl-Pyrrolidone) in 8:1:1 wt ratio, using THINKY ARE 250. The electrode coating was applied to the copper foil (current collector) using Dr. Blade's technique. The coated electrodes were then dried in a vacuum in two steps, first for 12 h at 70 °C and subsequently for 12 h at 120 °C. Finally, using CR2032 cells from MTI Corp., the electrodes were punched (diameter 12 mm) and half-cells were assembled. The coated copper foil was used as the anode, lithium foil as the cathode, Celgard 2400 was chosen as the separator, and Merck quality standard solution made of 1 M LiPF6 in EC-DMC (1:1) was preferred as the electrolyte (1.00028.1000). Half-cell assembly was completed in a glovebox with an Ar atmosphere (LABmaster Pro, MBRAUN, 0.1 ppm H2O and O2).

The galvanostatic performance of each cell was measured by Neware Battery Analyzer System: under a current load of 50 mA g − 1, each electrode was cycled between 10 mV-3 V vs. Li/Li+. The structural stability of the anode material was tested through a rate test by cycling the anode at different current loads of 100 mAg−1, 200 mAg−1, and 300 mAg−1 within the same voltage window. Then, to get an understanding of the lithiation mechanism of each electrode, cycling voltammetry (CV) was applied at a scan rate of 0.1 Vs−1 between cut-off voltages of 10 mV-3 V (vs Li/Li+) (Gamry Interface1000E). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to discuss the differentiation in the impedance upon the KOH activation process. In EIS measurement, to eliminate the interference issued from metallic Li, symmetric cells were prepared following the procedure suggested by Burns et al. [72]. The EIS spectra were recorded at 10 mV rms, from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz (Gamry Interface1000E).

CDs were prepared by simple carburization and hydrothermal reaction processes. The only reagent used in this study was watermelon seeds (in water) which act as a carbon source. The latter highlights the green aspect of this work. To investigate the effect of hydrothermal reaction parameters (the concentration of the solution, time, and temperature of the hydrothermal reaction) on the CD formation mechanism seven experiments were executed. Parametric details of the experiments are mentioned in Table 2.

To better understand the formation mechanism of the CD, it is crucial to know about the constituent of the seed (39.17% cellulose, 30% lignin, and approximately 20% hemicellulose) [51]. It has been summarized in the literature that the hydrothermal reaction of the biomass experiences chemical changes through condensation (which constitutes of hydrolysis, dehydration, and decarboxylation processes), polymerization, aromatization, and passivation [73–75]. The in-depth literature survey reveals that these reactions are not occurring stepwise but rather simultaneously.

During condensation reaction, in the hydrolysis step, ester and ether bonds, which are found in cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, are primarily broken. As a result, relatively smaller organic molecules such as phenol, fructose, saccharides, acetic acid, and formic acid are formed. Depending on the temperature of the reaction, these molecules may occasionally undergo further hydrolysis, or they experience dehydration. In dehydration, water molecules are extracted by removal of hydroxyl groups, which results in the loss of water molecules from seeds without changing their chemical compositions (see Eq. (2)). And, in decarboxylation, around 150 °C, the carbonyl and carboxyl groups break down, resulting in the formation of CO2 and CO, respectively. Formic acid's carboxyl group and the condensation reaction with the other ingredients are the main sources of CO2. Afterward, the solid residue gets easily polymerized, which is followed by hexagonal aromatic ring formation during the ‘aromatization’.

In this study, hemicellulose (found in high concentrations in the seeds of watermelons) has an inherent ability to produce aromatic compounds in the presence or absence of water. pH (basic environment), temperature, and time all affect aromatization [73,74]. Hence, once these aromatized polymers are formed, they get together to form clusters and passivate their surface with functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, etc. These clusters transform into small-sized dots at longer hydrothermal reaction duration [75]. The above-mentioned detailed method is graphically represented in Fig. 2 as a possible mechanism of CD generation.
(2)
4
(
C 6
H 10
O 5
)
n
↔ 10
H 2
O + 2
(
C 12
H 10
O 5
)
n

In this study, after each hydrothermal reaction, a faint yellow liquid containing CDs was obtained, and each sample was examined under UV radiation (Fig. 3 a-c). The broad shoulder band on the absorbance curve around 360 nm was observed for all samples (Fig. 3b). FTIR analysis of each solution revealed that the same functional group exists in all samples, independent of process parameters, which verified the explanation related to the CD formation (Supp. Figure S1).

Fig. 3b shows that when each dots solution was excited at 365 nm, it emited a broad emission spectrum centered at the wavelength of 455 nm (Fig. 3b, inset picture). But the intensity of the emission differs depending on the process parameters (Fig. 3b, inset picture).

Table 2 reveals that experiment 2 (H2) has the highest yield as it has the best combination of hydrothermal parameters. Because 200 °C was the most suited temperature for hydrothermal reaction of the substance containing cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose, akin to the literature [73]. Then, 2 hours’ reaction time was also adequate, since longer duration resulted in a greater degree of dehydration and decarboxylation, which led to lower yield. Moreover, a higher solid/liquid ratio in the reactor (concentration: 0.15 g/50 ml) exhibited a lower yield relative to 0.1 g/50 ml since at the end of 2 h, the structures formed in the reactor with a higher solid/liquid ratio may led further agglomeration and growth of carbon nanoparticles, resulting in a larger size than dots.

Then, as the highest CD formation's yield was found for H2, this sample was excited at different wavelengths ranging from 285 nm to 425 nm. A shift in the peak position of each emission curve (from 385 nm to 413 nm) was noted in Fig. 3c. This shift in the emission wavelength was explained based on the presence of the functional group C = O on the surface [76–78] of nanoparticles, as revealed by FTIR (Supp. Figure S1). The presence of such functionality on the surface resulted in numerous arrangements in the structure. These arrangements create more sub-levels of energy states and make numerous electronic transitions possible which gives rise to excitation-based emission [77,79]. This fact demonstrates the tunable emissive nature of CD which enables their use for bio imaging.

To obtain further insight knowledge about the morphology of carbon nanodots TEM analysis was carried out (Fig. 4 (a-b)). It was found that the CDs appear as spherical and/or polyhedral shapes with different sizes varying from 5 to 10 nm. Moreover, SAED revealed that the particles are not fully crystalline (Fig. 4c), which is consistent with the literature [43,80].

Fig. 5 (a - b) demonstrate SEM images of the carburized powders: without KOH treatment: sample 1, and with KOH-treatment: sample 2, respectively. The carburized powder has dense granular morphologies before the KOH activation, however, after the KOH activation, the powder exhibits considerable porosity formation. TEM image of Sample 2 revealed this porous surface morphology: the surveillance of interconnected nanopores was noted on Fig. 5(c, d).

XRD results showed that before activation the peaks matched with those of graphite (PDF 89–8490) but after activation the degree of amorphization was increased as merely two broad peaks at 2θ = 24° and around 42° were noted in Fig. 6 a, akin to the literature [54,71].

The Raman spectra of sample 1 and sample 2 are given in Fig. 6b. The appearance of d-Band at 1364 cm−1 is related to the presence of imperfection and disordered structure of carbonaceous structure and G-band at 1594 cm−1 was attributed to vibrations of carbon atoms in graphitic planner sheet structure having sp2 hybridized electronic configuration [60,81]. The ratio of integral intensities of d-band and G-band (ID/IG) is an important term to discuss the degree of disorder-ness in carbon-based substances: the higher the value more disordered the structure will be. Here in, the value of the integral intensity ratio of sample 2 (ID/IG = 0.98) is higher than that of sample 1 (ID/IG = 0.93). This indicates that sample 2 has higher amorphization with a greater number of edges and defects than sample 1, as expected [56,60,81].

Moreover, the FTIR results of sample 1 and sample 2 are displayed in Fig. 6c. As prepared sample 1 showed two major peaks of absorption at 1636.5 cm−1 and 3317 cm−1 which respectively belong to C = O and OH stretching. However, following the activation procedure, the FTIR curve smooths out and the previously discovered bumps vanish. This demonstrates the elimination of the volatile material that was present prior to activation and the occurrence of aromatization during activation.

The structural characterization results (Figs. 5 and 6) showed that the mechanism of activation through KOH involves three steps: first chemical activation, which involves the etching of carbonaceous substance through potassium species. Then in the second step, physical activation was realized by the elimination of CO2 and H2O molecules which enhanced porosity generation (Eqs. (3) and (4)) because during activation, KOH consumed some of the carbon to form K2CO3, which got dissolved in water and was removed from the powder through washing.
(3)
4 K O H + C →
K 2
C
O 3
(
water
soluble
)
+
H 2
O + 2
H 2
(4)
2 K O H + C +
H 2
O →
K 2
C
O 3
(
water
soluble
)
+ 2
H 2

And finally, in the third step, potassium penetrated in the carbon structure and enlarged the interlayer structure of graphite [71,82,83]. This characteristic is useful for a material to be used as anode material in lithium-ion batteries due to the larger interlayer spacing in activated carbon. It favors the reversible intercalation of Li+ ions and keeps the structure prominently stable, hence resulting the enhanced electrochemical performance [71,84–87].

A similar trend had been observed and disclosed by Li et al. [88] in their study. They claimed that the pores increase the specific surface area, which facilitates deep immersion of the electrolyte and effectively shortens the path of ion diffusion for the accumulation of lithium. And, this ultimately led to an increase in the number of electrochemical reactions together with a consequent increase in capacity [54,58,81]. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and information about the porosity of sample 2 were evaluated by the N2-sorption isotherm. The specific surface area was found to be 1151.273 m2 g − 1, with a pore volume of 0.5907 cm3 g − 1 and a pore diameter of 6.159 Å (Fig. 6d and e). Such morphology was believed to promote lithiation reaction at the electrode/electrolyte interface by providing a high number of active sites for lithium to react with.

Fig. 7 exhibits galvanostatic cycle performances of sample 2 over 200 cycles. A scrutiny look at the voltage-capacity curve of sample 2 revealed that around 1.3–0.9 V a plateau was noted related to the electrolyte decomposition and formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) [71,89]. Such a plateau was not significant in the discharge curve of sample 1 (See Supp. Fig S2: voltage-capacity curve of Sample 1). The high capacity delivered by sample 2 was believed to be related to the increased surface area due to enhanced porosity and a higher number of defects formed after activation which provided a greater number of active sites for electrochemical reaction and hence an increased amount of lithium insertion [58,81,90]. The large capacity loss after the first cycle could be ascribed to a large amount of SEI formation due to the improved surface area of the electrode active material, as well as the entrapped lithium ions which intercalated deep inside the carbon structure and then could not be fully abstained during charging which resulted in irreversible capacity loss [58,81,91]. After stable SEI formation in the first cycles, it was seen that the capacity progressed steadily around 360 mAh g − 1 with >98% coulombic efficiency. The comparison of the electrochemical performance obtained from this study with the others mentioned in the literature is given in Table 1. The improved performance obtained in this study was attributed to the enhanced degree of porosity and structural disorderness resulting after the activation process.

The cyclic voltammogram profile of sample 2 is also illustrated in Fig. 7(c). In sample 2 two cathodic peaks appeared at 1.5 V and 1.25 V, these two can be attributed to a reaction between carbon and lithium ions (following the reaction given in Eq. (5)), electrolyte decomposition, and formation of SEI, respectively [71,81,89,92]. However, a couple of humps were obtained at around 1.25 V and 2.5 V, these were also due to the deintercalation of lithium ions from the deep inside of the porous structure of carbon [60,71,81,89]. Moreover, the overlap in the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th CV profiles showed remarkable structural stability and conveyed another idea that the formation of SEI layers also assisted in upholding structural integrity in cycling [71,81]. However, the CV profile of sample 1 was unlike that of sample 2. No significant peaks appeared showing the absence of lithium reaction with carbon (see Fig.S2).
(5)
L
i
+
+ e + n C ↔ L i
C n

Fig. 7d shows the EIS results of sample 1 and sample 2. It can be seen that charge transfer resistance has decreased after activation. This reflected the availability of more active sites for lithium to react within carbon (in sample 2). Activation resulted in the enhancement in the surface area as shown in the SEM & TEM image of sample 2. A larger surface area offered more reaction sites eventually reducing the charge transfer resistance. It can clearly be observed from the EIS graphs of the two samples that diffusion resistance of sample 2 was much lesser than sample 1, hence proving that the porous structure, resulting from activation, shortened the lithium diffusion path [93]. Moreover, to assess the structural integrity of the anode material the anode was tested at different high loads (i.e., 100 mAg−1, 200 mAg−1, and 300 mAg−1) and results are given in Fig. 7e. It can evidently be observed that at a higher load there was an obvious capacity decrease but even at a load of 300 mAg−1, the anode material delivered a capacity of ∼200 mAhg−1.

In this study, an approach to valorize biowaste (watermelon seeds) has been presented through its dual-purpose utilization. Firstly, a combination of carburizing and a hydrothermal reaction was utilized to fabricate carbon dots via a green and effective procedure. The maximum quantum yield is observed when reaction time and temperature were selected as 2 h and 200 °C, respectively, and a solution concentration of 0.1 g / 50 ml (sample 1 in distilled water) was used. The resulting dots radiate high-intensity fluorescence centered at 458 nm when excited with 365 nm. These absorbance and emission results indicate that the synthesized carbon dots have a potential to be used in biomedical applications like cell imaging etc. Secondly, a combination of carburizing and activation was applied to produce low carbon footprint electrode material for lithium ion batteries. This electrode shows 811 mAh g − 1 as the 1st discharge capacity and delivers around ∼360 mAh g − 1 capacity after 200th cycle. These results prove that by accurate material selection and process design, biowaste could be used as initial material for the synthesis of high value added products used in various applications.

Humza Ashraf: Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. B. Deniz Karahan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Billur Deniz Karahan reports financial support was provided by Istanbul Medipol University. Billur Deniz Karahan reports a relationship with Istanbul Medipol University that includes: employment between 2017 and 2022. Istanbul Medipol University sponsored the realization and characterization of the experiments. Istanbul Technical University is the current affiliation of the corresponding author.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge Istanbul Medipol University Regenerative and Restorative Medicine Research Center for allowing the use of SEM and XRD in characterizations and Konya Selçuk University (as well as Özkan Üzüm) for TEM analysis. The authors also thank Alper Yeşilyurt (Arçelik) for the XRF and Raman analyses and Dr. Nilay Beğiç (Biruni University) for providing quinine sulfate.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2023.112492.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifaceted applications of green carbon dots synthesized from renewable sources
Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Tejwan and S.K. Saha, "Multifaceted applications of green carbon dots synthesized from renewable sources," Adv. Colloid Interface Sci., vol. 275, p. 102046, 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
